summary: Gruber argues that Donald Trump's 2016 campaign strategy makes more sense if his goal was never to win the presidency but to build a media empire. Drawing on claims from former Trump strategist Stephanie Cegielski, Michael Moore, and reporting from the New York Times, Gruber presents evidence that Trump's original goal was merely to finish second and boost his brand. The hiring of Breitbart's Steve Bannon and consultation with Roger Ailes suggest Trump is building an audience for a right-wing media outlet rather than a winning electoral coalition. Gruber contends that Trump's refusal to pivot to the center is rational if viewed as audience-building for a post-election media venture targeting voters who think Fox News is too mainstream.
key_points: Trump's campaign strategy of doubling down on nationalism rather than pivoting to the center is irrational for winning but rational for building a loyal media audience
Former Trump strategist Cegielski claimed the original goal was only to poll in double digits and finish second — a brand-building exercise, not a serious campaign
The Kasich VP offer — where Trump's son said the VP would handle all domestic and foreign policy — reveals Trump had no interest in actually governing
Hiring Bannon and consulting Ailes signals media-mogul aspirations: building a network to the right of Fox News
Trump isn't trying to appeal to more people (how you win elections) but to appeal more intensely to his existing base (how you build a media audience)
money_quotes: He is, fundamentally, a self-promotional candidate. He's doing it to promote the Trump brand and increase his personal celebrity.
I think Trump finds being a candidate for president to be a lot of fun. He loves the big crowds and the sound of his own voice.
I think he'd find actually being president to be terribly boring.

Trump isn't trying to appeal to more people, which is how you win elections. He's trying to appeal more to the people who already support him. That's how you might build an audience for an 'alt-right' media company.
The idea that the Republican nominee for president doesn't actually want to be president is outlandish. But Donald Trump is outlandish.
Here is a short list of adjectives I personally would use to describe Trump: reprehensible, bigoted, reckless, obnoxious, selfish, incurious, under-informed. But one word I wouldn't use is stupid.
If Trump is trying to win the election, what he's doing the last few weeks doesn't make any sense. But if his goal is not to win, then maybe he's crazy like a fox.
key_insight: Trump's seemingly self-destructive campaign strategy becomes rational if his true goal is not the presidency but building an intensely loyal audience for a post-election media empire to the right of Fox News.
